Three ingredients: (1) a **registry stage flip** — promotion/demotion is a metadata write, milliseconds, so "production" re-points to the previous version instantly; (2) **traffic-shift mechanics** — the serving layer (service mesh / load balancer / router weights) re-routes to the already-running old version, no rebuild, no redeploy; (3) a **warm standby** — the previous model stays loaded and healthy so the flip incurs no cold-start. The 90 seconds is detection + automated gate decision + traffic drain, *not* a deploy. If rollback means "rebuild and redeploy the old version," it's minutes-to-hours and the opener is a lie.

> **Q: Shadow vs canary — when each?** → Shadow first to validate latency/errors/output with zero user risk (you can't measure outcomes); canary next to measure real quality on a small slice with auto-rollback.
> **Q: Why isn't an A/B test a deployment *safety* mechanism?** → A/B measures business lift over days with statistics; safety needs fast detection and rollback on the order of minutes — that's canary + automated gates.
> **Q: How is rollback actually 90 seconds?** → Registry stage flip (metadata write) + traffic re-point at a warm-standby old version that's still loaded — detection and traffic drain dominate, there's no rebuild or redeploy.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 13. LLMOps — the layer that bolts on top

LLMOps inherits everything above and adds problems that don't exist for a sklearn model: prompts are code, outputs are non-deterministic and open-ended, evaluation is fuzzy, and tokens cost money per request.

**Prompt versioning & registry.** Prompts are deployable artifacts — version them, register them, tie a prod prompt to its eval scores. A prompt change is a deploy and goes through the same gate as a model change.

**Eval-in-CI + regression suites.** The LLM analogue of the test pyramid. On every prompt/model/RAG change, run a **golden dataset** (curated input→expected-property pairs) through an eval pipeline; block the merge if scores regress.

```python
# eval-in-CI: golden set + judge, gate the merge
def eval_gate(generate, golden, judge, baseline):
    scores = []
    for case in golden:                      # curated, versioned, frozen
        out = generate(case["input"])
        scores.append(judge(out, case["criteria"]))   # LLM-judge or rule/regex
    mean = sum(scores) / len(scores)
    assert mean >= baseline - 0.02, f"eval regressed: {mean} < {baseline}"
```

**Model gateway / routing.** One internal endpoint in front of many providers/models: route by cost/quality/latency, fail over, enforce rate limits, inject auth, centralize logging. Lets you swap GPT↔Claude↔open-weights without touching app code. Two responsibilities interviewers add: **secret/API-key management** — provider keys live in the gateway (a secrets manager with rotation), never in app code or client, so a key rotates in one place and never leaks to the edge; and **multi-tenancy isolation** — the gateway enforces *per-tenant* rate and cost budgets, so one tenant can't exhaust quota or run up another's bill, with per-tenant logging for chargeback.

    request ─> [gateway: authn, per-tenant rate/cost limit, route, inject provider key]
                    │
              [semantic cache] ──hit──> response
                    │miss
              [guardrails-in] ─> LLM (routed) ─> [guardrails-out] ─> log tokens/cost/tenant ─> response

> **Q: Why version prompts, and through what process?** → A prompt is behavior-defining code; version + register it and push changes through eval-in-CI with a golden-set gate, exactly like a model.
> **Q: What's a golden dataset?** → A curated, frozen set of representative inputs with expected properties/criteria, used as the regression suite so every change is scored against a stable bar.
> **Q: Two gateway responsibilities beyond routing?** → Secret management (provider keys in a rotating secrets store, never at the edge) and per-tenant rate/cost isolation so one tenant can't starve or bill another.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 14. LLM evaluation rigor — the judge is a model, treat it like one

"LLM-as-judge" in §13's code is convenient and *biased*. A senior candidate names the failure modes and the calibration.

**Judge pitfalls:**

- **Position bias** — pairwise judges favor the first (or second) answer regardless of quality; mitigate by swapping order and averaging.
- **Verbosity bias** — judges reward longer, more confident answers even when wrong.
- **Self-preference** — a judge favors outputs from its own model family.
- **Judge-model drift** — the provider silently updates the judge model; your scores move with no code change. Pin the judge version and re-baseline on change.

**Calibration & methodology:**

- **Human calibration** — periodically score a sample by humans and check the judge correlates; an uncalibrated judge is a vibe.
- **Pairwise vs pointwise** — pairwise (A vs B, which is better) is more reliable than pointwise (rate 1–10); humans and judges are better at comparison than absolute scoring.
- **Inter-rater agreement** — for human eval, measure κ across annotators; for judge-vs-human, measure correlation/agreement, and report it.

**Offline vs online eval:**

| | Offline (pre-ship) | Online (production) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Data** | Frozen golden set, public benchmarks | Live traffic, real users |
| **Signal** | Regression gate, leaderboard | A/B on business KPI, thumbs up/down, escalation rate |
| **Catches** | Did the change break known cases | Did it help real users, real-world distribution |

Offline keeps you from shipping a regression; online tells you if it *mattered*. You need both — a green offline suite with no production feedback loop is flying blind.

**Evaluating agents / tool-use** (not just single-turn generation): score the **trajectory**, not only the final string — did it pick the right tool, with valid arguments, recover from a tool error, and avoid loops? Metrics: tool-selection accuracy, argument validity, task-completion (did the end state satisfy the goal), step efficiency.

```python
# agent eval: grade the trajectory, not just the final answer
def grade_trajectory(steps, goal):
    return {
        "tool_acc":   mean(s.tool == s.expected_tool for s in steps),
        "args_valid": mean(validate_args(s.tool, s.args) for s in steps),
        "completed":  goal_satisfied(steps[-1].state, goal),  # end-state check
        "efficiency": expected_steps(goal) / max(len(steps), 1),  # <1 = wandered
    }
```

> **Q: Name three LLM-judge biases and a mitigation each.** → Position (swap order, average), verbosity (penalize length / rubric on correctness not length), self-preference (use a different judge family) — and pin the judge version against silent drift.
> **Q: Pairwise vs pointwise judging?** → Pairwise (which of A/B is better) is more reliable than pointwise absolute scores; judges and humans compare better than they rate.
> **Q: Offline vs online LLM eval?** → Offline = frozen golden set/benchmarks gating regressions pre-ship; online = live A/B, thumbs, escalation rate telling you if it helped real users — you need both.
> **Q: How do you eval an agent vs a single completion?** → Grade the trajectory — tool-selection accuracy, argument validity, error recovery, end-state task completion, step efficiency — not just the final text.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 15. RAG evaluation — split retrieval from generation, track the artifacts

§13 named faithfulness loosely; production RAG eval is more disciplined. Eval retrieval and generation **separately**, because a RAG regression is usually a *retrieval* regression masquerading as a generation one.

| Layer | Metric | Question |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Retrieval** | **context recall** | Did we retrieve *all* the chunks needed to answer? (miss → guaranteed hallucination) |
| **Retrieval** | **context precision** | Are the retrieved chunks *relevant*, ranked high? (noise → distracted generation) |
| **Generation** | **faithfulness / groundedness** | Is every claim in the answer supported by retrieved context? |
| **Generation** | **answer relevance** | Does the answer actually address the question? |

Frameworks that compute these: **RAGAS** and **TruLens** (the "RAG triad": context relevance, groundedness, answer relevance). The key distinction interviewers want: **context recall vs context precision** — recall = did you get the needed chunk at all; precision = is what you got relevant and not padded with junk. Low recall caps your ceiling; low precision degrades the generation.

**Track the RAG inputs as versioned artifacts.** The **embedding model** and the **chunking strategy** (size/overlap/splitter) are part of the system — change either and retrieval quality moves. Version them and run the RAG eval suite on any change, exactly like a model bump. A silent embedding-model upgrade is a regression waiting to happen.

> **Q: Context recall vs context precision?** → Recall = did retrieval surface all chunks needed to answer (miss → forced hallucination); precision = are the retrieved chunks relevant and well-ranked (junk → distracted generation).
> **Q: Most common RAG eval mistake?** → Evaluating end-to-end only and blaming the LLM for hallucination when the retriever never surfaced the answer chunk — split retrieval vs generation metrics.
> **Q: Why version the embedding model and chunking config?** → They're load-bearing RAG components; changing chunk size or embeddings shifts retrieval quality, so treat them as artifacts and re-run RAG eval on any change.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 16. LLM security & guardrails — prompt injection is OWASP LLM #1

"Guardrails as a service" is the *mechanism*; the *threat model* is the interview. Centralized input/output filtering belongs at the gateway/sidecar (PII, toxicity, schema/JSON validity, topic bounds) — but the headline risk is **prompt injection**, #1 on the OWASP LLM Top 10.

- **Direct prompt injection / jailbreak** — the user crafts input that overrides your system prompt ("ignore previous instructions…"). Jailbreak taxonomies: role-play ("you are DAN"), obfuscation (base64/leetspeak to slip filters), many-shot (flood context with fake examples), prompt-leak (extract the system prompt).
- **Indirect prompt injection** — the malicious instruction rides in *retrieved content* (a web page, a RAG document, an email the agent reads). The model can't tell data from instructions, so attacker-controlled content in your RAG corpus becomes an instruction. This is the genuinely scary one for agents with tools.

**Defenses (layered, none sufficient alone):**

- **Input filtering** — detect known jailbreak/injection patterns at the gateway.
- **Privilege separation** — the model never gets raw credentials or unguarded tools; tool calls go through an allowlist with their own authz, so a hijacked model can't exfiltrate or escalate (least privilege for the agent).
- **Output structured-validation + retry loop** — force JSON/schema output, validate, and on failure re-prompt with the error rather than passing malformed/unsafe output downstream.
- **Content provenance** — mark retrieved/third-party content as untrusted in the prompt and instruct the model to treat it as data, not commands (mitigation, not a guarantee).

```python
# output guardrail: structured-validation with a bounded retry loop
def guarded_generate(prompt, schema, max_retries=2):
    for _ in range(max_retries + 1):
        out = llm(prompt)
        ok, err = validate_schema(out, schema)   # JSON shape + value bounds
        if ok and not contains_pii(out) and not policy_violation(out):
            return out
        prompt += f"\n\nYour previous output was invalid: {err}. Return valid output."
    raise GuardrailFailure()                      # fail closed, don't ship bad output
```

> **Q: What is indirect prompt injection and why is it worse than direct?** → The attack instruction hides in content the model *retrieves* (RAG doc, web page, email), so a user who never touches your prompt can hijack the model via your corpus — and the model can't separate data from instructions.
> **Q: OWASP LLM #1, and one structural defense?** → Prompt injection; defend with privilege separation — the model gets no raw secrets and only allowlisted tools with their own authz, so a hijack can't escalate.
> **Q: How does an output guardrail use a retry loop?** → Force schema output, validate shape/values/policy, and on failure re-prompt with the error (bounded retries); fail closed rather than passing malformed/unsafe output downstream.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 17. Inference cost levers & LLM observability

Training GPU cost is §18; *inference* cost is a different, per-request bill, and observability for LLMs is tracing, not just a latency histogram.

**Inference cost levers (right-size before you optimize):**

- **Right-size the model per task** — don't route a classification to a frontier model; a small/distilled model or a cheaper tier handles most traffic, escalate only hard cases (model cascade).
- **Prompt caching / KV-cache reuse** — providers cache the prefix (system prompt + few-shot) so repeated prefixes aren't re-encoded; structure prompts with the static part first to maximize cache hits.
- **Continuous (in-flight) batching** — the serving engine (vLLM/TGI) packs many requests' tokens into each GPU step and admits new requests mid-batch, vs naive static batching that waits for the slowest sequence — the single biggest serving-throughput lever.
- **Speculative decoding** — a small draft model proposes several tokens, the big model verifies them in one pass; same output distribution, fewer big-model steps.
- **Quantization for serving** — INT8/INT4/FP8 weights cut memory and raise throughput with measured quality loss (eval-gate it).
- **Distillation** — train a small model on the big model's outputs for the specific task; ship the small one.
- **Semantic caching** — cache by *embedding similarity* of the prompt, not exact string; a paraphrase hits. Gotcha: too-loose a threshold returns a wrong-but-similar cached answer — tune the threshold and scope keys per tenant/context.

**LLM observability — trace every request as a span tree:**

    request
      └─ retrieval  (query, k, chunk ids, latency)
         └─ prompt assembly  (final prompt, token count)
            └─ completion  (model, input/output tokens, $, latency)
               └─ guardrail  (pass/fail, which rule)

You need **per-request tracing** (retrieval → prompt → completion → tokens/cost), not just aggregate latency, because debugging a bad answer means replaying *that* request's exact context. Capture **production feedback** (thumbs up/down, edits, escalations) and pipe negative examples back into the golden eval set — production failures become tomorrow's regression tests. Track **hallucination rate in prod** (sampled groundedness checks on live traffic), token/cost per feature/tenant, and cache-hit rate. Tools: **Langfuse**, **Arize Phoenix**, **WhyLabs**.

> **Q: Biggest single lever for LLM serving throughput?** → Continuous/in-flight batching (vLLM/TGI) — admit and pack requests per GPU step instead of waiting for the slowest sequence in a static batch.
> **Q: What does prompt caching exploit and how do you structure prompts for it?** → Reuse of the encoded prefix (KV cache); put the static system/few-shot content first so the cacheable prefix is shared across requests.
> **Q: Speculative decoding — free lunch?** → It cuts latency (small model drafts, big model verifies in one pass) with *identical* output distribution; cost is the extra draft model and complexity, not quality.
> **Q: What does LLM tracing capture that a latency dashboard misses?** → The per-request span tree — retrieval hits, the exact assembled prompt, tokens, cost, guardrail outcome — so you can replay and debug a specific bad answer, not just see an aggregate.
> **Q: Where do production-feedback signals go?** → Thumbs/edits/escalations feed back into the golden eval set so real failures become regression tests, and sampled groundedness gives a live hallucination rate.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 18. GPU economics, on-call, governance & compliance

The operational tail interviewers use to check you've actually run this in production.

- **GPU cost/scheduling** — GPUs are the bill. Bin-pack jobs, use **MIG**/time-slicing to share a card, spot/preemptible for training (checkpoint to survive eviction), autoscale-to-zero for spiky inference, quantize/batch to raise throughput-per-GPU. Queue training; never let notebooks idle-hold an A100.
- **Incident response / on-call for ML** — runbooks for *model* incidents: drift alert → is it data or world change; quality drop → rollback to champion first, diagnose second; bad-data-poisoned model → roll back model *and* quarantine the data version. The first action is always **mitigate (rollback)**, not debug.
- **Governance / audit** — model cards (intended use, limits, training data, metrics by slice), lineage for "why did this model deny this loan," approval gates on promotion, immutable logs of predictions for contestability, and for LLMs: PII handling, data-retention, and which provider saw what.

**Compliance specifics (presales/architect-level probes):**

- **EU AI Act risk tiers** — *unacceptable* (banned, e.g. social scoring), *high-risk* (credit, hiring, biometrics — conformity assessment, logging, human oversight required), *limited-risk* (transparency: tell users they're talking to AI), *minimal*. Know which tier your system lands in; high-risk carries hard obligations.
- **GDPR right-to-explanation** (Art. 22) — a person subject to a solely-automated decision can demand meaningful information about the logic; ties back to lineage + model cards + interpretability, and may force a human-in-the-loop on automated denials.
- **Data / model residency** — regulated data (and sometimes the model and inference) must stay in-region; for LLMs this dictates which provider/region endpoint you may call, and whether you can use a US-hosted API at all — a routing constraint in the gateway, not an afterthought.

> **Q: Three levers to cut GPU cost without hurting quality?** → Spot/preemptible with checkpointing for training; MIG/time-slicing + batching to raise per-GPU utilization for serving; autoscale-to-zero for spiky inference.
> **Q: First action when a model quality alert fires at 3am?** → Mitigate — roll back to the champion — then diagnose; don't debug a live regression.
> **Q: What changes if your system is EU AI Act "high-risk"?** → Mandatory conformity assessment, logging, human oversight, and documentation (data/metrics by slice) before deployment — vs limited-risk which only needs AI-disclosure transparency.
> **Q: How does data residency constrain an LLM architecture?** → It dictates which provider/region endpoints the gateway may route to (or forces self-hosting in-region); you can't call a US API on EU-resident regulated data.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 19. Interview Q&A (rapid fire)

> **Q: One-sentence definition of MLOps level 2?** → The training *pipeline itself* ships through CI/CD, so changes to the ML system auto-test and auto-deploy, with CT running it on triggers.
> **Q: CI vs CT?** → CI tests code/data/model on change; CT (re)trains the model on a data/drift/schedule trigger and emits a registry candidate.
> **Q: Why hash data instead of storing its path?** → A path is mutable; only a content hash proves *which bytes* a run trained on — required for reproducibility and lineage.
> **Q: Offline vs online store difference that matters?** → Offline = high-throughput history for training; online = low-latency single-entity lookup for serving; same feature definition materialized to both kills skew.
> **Q: Backfill precondition?** → Idempotent, execution-date-parametrized tasks — otherwise reruns double-write or use `now()` and drift.
> **Q: Champion/challenger in one line?** → Prod model (champion) keeps serving; challengers run in shadow/canary and only get promoted when they beat the gate metric.
> **Q: Where do guardrails belong?** → At the gateway/sidecar as a shared service, applied to input and output, not re-coded per app.
> **Q: Pre-merge vs post-merge in an ML CI pipeline?** → Pre-merge: cheap, fast checks + smoke-train on a sample; post-merge: the expensive full train and full eval — you can't gate code review on GPU-hours.
> **Q: Safe alternative to deploying a pickle?** → ONNX/TorchScript for the graph, Safetensors for weights, served behind Triton/TF Serving/TorchServe/BentoML — no arbitrary-code-on-load.
> **Q: The drift you can't detect from inputs alone?** → Concept drift (`P(Y|X)`) — it needs ground-truth labels; covariate drift (`P(X)`) is label-free detectable.
> **Q: Catastrophic forgetting — when does it bite?** → Warm-start/incremental retraining on new data overwrites previously-learned patterns; full retrain avoids it.
> **Q: OWASP LLM Top 10 #1?** → Prompt injection, with indirect injection (via retrieved RAG/web content) the nastiest for tool-using agents.
> **Q: Context recall vs precision in RAG?** → Recall = got all needed chunks; precision = retrieved chunks are relevant — low recall forces hallucination, low precision distracts generation.
> **Q: One LLM-judge bias and its fix?** → Position bias — swap A/B order and average; also pin the judge model version against silent drift.
> **Q: Biggest LLM serving throughput lever?** → Continuous/in-flight batching (vLLM/TGI).
> **Q: Why is "we retrain nightly via cron" a red flag?** → No validation gate, no trigger logic, no registry candidate — it can overwrite prod with a model trained on poisoned/drifted data.
> **Q: What does a model gateway buy you?** → Provider abstraction, routing/failover, central rate-limiting, per-tenant cost isolation, secret management, cost logging — swap models without app changes.
> **Q: How is rollback 90 seconds and not 30 minutes?** → Registry stage flip + traffic re-point to a warm-standby old version — detection and drain dominate, no rebuild/redeploy.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 20. Role lens

- **ML Engineer** — owns the training pipeline as code: CT triggers, the test pyramid (esp. model invariance/slice gates), feature definitions and point-in-time joins, reproducibility (pin code/data/env/seed), the drift taxonomy and retrain strategy (warm-start vs full, catastrophic forgetting). Expected to explain skew, leakage, and label latency cold.
- **MLOps Engineer** — owns the platform: registry + stages, packaging/serving formats (ONNX/Triton, no pickles), orchestration (idempotency/backfills), deployment strategies and automated rollback mechanics, feature-store freshness/dual-write, GPU scheduling/cost, data-validation gates, on-call runbooks and governance/lineage. The "level 2 pipeline" and the 90-second rollback are your deliverables.
- **AI Engineer** — owns the LLMOps layer: prompt registry, eval-in-CI with golden sets, LLM-judge calibration, RAG eval (context recall vs precision, RAGAS/TruLens), prompt-injection defenses and output guardrails, the model gateway (routing, secrets, per-tenant isolation), inference-cost levers (continuous batching, prompt caching, speculative decoding, distillation), and observability/tracing (Langfuse/Phoenix) with production-feedback loops. Expected to make non-deterministic systems testable, safe, and cheap.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 21. Gotchas interviewers use to find your level

- Calling CI/CD "level 2" without realizing **the pipeline**, not the model, is the shipped artifact.
- Proposing to **full-train on every PR** — no notion of smoke-train pre-merge vs full-train post-merge, or of promoting one artifact across envs instead of rebuilding.
- Saying "we version the model" but not the **data, environment, or seeds** — partial reproducibility is none.
- Naming **"deploy the pickle"** with no awareness of the RCE risk or the ONNX/TorchScript/Safetensors + Triton alternatives.
- Describing a feature store as "just a cache" — missing **training-serving skew**, **point-in-time correctness**, **freshness/TTL**, and the **dual-write** problem as the actual reasons it exists.
- Joining latest feature values into a training set — **label leakage** that inflates offline metrics and dies in prod.
- Saying "trigger on performance decay" with **no answer for label latency** — you can't measure live accuracy when labels land weeks later; name the proxies.
- Conflating **covariate and concept drift**, or claiming you can detect concept drift without labels.
- "We retrain on a cron" with **no validation gate**, or warm-starting forever with **no awareness of catastrophic forgetting**, or no story for **where labels come from**.
- Treating data validation as a **warning** instead of a **pipeline-halting gate**.
- Confusing **shadow** (no user impact, no outcome signal) with **canary** (small real impact, measurable) — or calling an A/B test a safety mechanism — or claiming "90-second rollback" with no warm-standby/registry-flip mechanism behind it.
- Forgetting **idempotency**, then proposing backfills that double-count.
- Trusting **LLM-as-judge** blind — no awareness of position/verbosity/self-preference bias, judge-model drift, or human calibration.
- Evaluating RAG **end-to-end only**, no context-recall-vs-precision split, no versioning of the embedding model / chunking config.
- No mention of **prompt injection** (direct *or* indirect via RAG content) when asked about LLM security; no output structured-validation/retry loop; no privilege separation for tool-using agents.
- Talking inference cost as "use a smaller model" only — missing **continuous batching, prompt/KV caching, speculative decoding, quantization, distillation**.
- LLM observability as a **latency dashboard** with no per-request tracing, no token/cost-per-tenant, no production-feedback loop into the eval set, no in-prod hallucination tracking.
- No **automated rollback** — "we'd redeploy the old version manually" means minutes-to-hours of bad predictions.
- Treating prompts as config, not **versioned, eval-gated artifacts**; gateway with no **secret rotation** or **per-tenant cost isolation**.
- "Governance" as a buzzword with no **EU AI Act risk tier**, **GDPR right-to-explanation**, or **data/model residency** awareness.

## 🎓 Part II complete — handbook fully expanded

That closes **Part II — LLM & GenAI Deep Dive** (Ch 14–24), synthesized and de-duplicated from the two reference handbooks. Combined with **Part I** (Ch 00–13), the handbook now spans the full ML / MLOps / AI-Engineer interview surface:

- **Part I:** roles, SWE foundations, classical ML + DL, RAG/agents overview, infra, fine-tuning, K8s, observability, system design, data center, certs, behavioral, coding rounds.
- **Part II:** tokenization, embeddings, transformer internals, pretraining/model families, classification & topic discovery, deep RAG, prompting/orchestration, multimodal, the decoding/inference pipeline, optimization math, and advanced architectures/deployment.
- **Part III:** advanced deep dives — fine-tuning (adapter zoo, preference-optimization family, multi-adapter serving), production agents (MCP, reflection, agent eval, guardrails, tracing), and the full MLOps/LLMOps lifecycle.

**Drill it:** turn each chapter's Q&A into `/anki` decks for spaced repetition, and re-read the **gotchas** the night before an interview. Read it in-app at **`/handbook`** (the sidebar auto-lists every chapter). Good luck. 🚀
